content.assessment: Networked retention is the proposal most explicitly aligned with the historical Hungarian pattern that kept within-school segregation low by avoiding closures, while acknowledging that this approach never solved between-settlement segregation and territorial concentration of disadvantage. Its equity promise depends heavily on whether pooled specialist and pedagogical resources reach the most disadvantaged villages equitably, or whether hub siting reproduces existing advantage geographies; the evidence base for success (a single case) is thin.
opportunities: Hub design could be deliberately targeted at the least-developed regions and highest early-school-leaving areas to counteract, rather than reproduce, existing distributional gaps (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08)
Networked teacher-sharing could disproportionately benefit disadvantaged schools that currently have 2-3 times higher rates of unqualified teachers (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09)
strengths: Explicitly aims to preserve the documented historical benefit of limiting within-school segregation by avoiding outright closures (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)
Combining kindergarten and primary provision in hubs could address compounding early-years and primary access gaps found in the same disadvantaged villages (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)

Cross-school professional networks and multigrade training models offer a route to mitigate the acute rural teacher shortage and unqualified-teacher concentration without requiring physical consolidation (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09)
threats: If hub siting follows existing settlement hierarchies, it could recreate a milder version of the sorting problem it aims to avoid
Villages lacking any school already number well beyond a third of settlements, and networked models may not extend to the most disconnected, worst-off villages absent deliberate targeting
weaknesses: Between-settlement segregation and growing territorial concentration of Roma populations in ghetto schools, concentrated in North-East Hungary, are not directly addressed by a networking model that keeps villages' schools intact (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)

Reliance documented elsewhere on rare local social capital suggests networked retention could favor villages that already have organizational advantages, reproducing unequal access to the model itself
